mappings.rationale: Examines stratigraphic principles, geochemistry, and the rock record that form the foundation of interpreting the geologic record (A-E).
Directly addresses fossilization processes (B.1), the fossil record from the Burgess Shale onward (B.2), and the use of fossils for stratigraphic correlation (B.4).
Explores the Phanerozoic fossil record (B.2.b), mass extinctions (B.3), the origin of life (B.2.a), and the molecular clock for dating evolutionary events.
Examines sedimentary, igneous, and metamorphic rocks and their depositional environments (A.1), directly relevant to understanding layered rocks of the Earth's crust.
Addresses dinosaurs and other fossil reptiles (B.2.b.x) and the iridium anomaly marking the Cretaceous mass extinction (B.3).
Directly addresses mass extinctions and background extinctions as revealed in the fossil record (B.3), including the Pleistocene megafaunal extinction.
Explains radioactive decay principles (D.2.a) and radiometric dating methods (D.2) used to determine the absolute age of rocks and the Earth (D.4.b).
Explores carbon dating and isotopic methods (D.2.d) for determining ages of rocks and meteorites (D.4.b) and rates of seafloor spreading (D.4.c).
Essential for paleogeographical interpretation (E) and determination of seafloor spreading rates from the geologic record (D.4.c).
Addresses radioactive decay for age determination (D.4.b) and the geological processes that produce the layered rock record (A).
Illuminates the concept of fossil succession (A.2.c) and the appearance and disappearance of species in the fossil record (B.3).
Examines mineral composition of rocks including calcite, feldspar, quartz, and clay minerals (A.1.a-b), essential for identifying sedimentary and carbonate rock types.
Explores geophysical methods including paleomagnetism for stratigraphic correlation and determination of seafloor spreading rates (D.4.c, E).
Addresses radiometric dating of landforms and the geomorphological processes that shape depositional environments (A.1) and paleogeography (E).
Explores volcanic rocks (A.1.c) and pyroclastic deposits that form important marker beds for stratigraphic correlation.
Traces the fossil record of mammals (B.2.b.x), their evolution and radiation through geological time, and their use as index fossils.
Fossil cnidarians and reef-building corals (B.2.b.iv) serve as important indicators in the paleontological record.
Surveys the major invertebrate phyla -- Cnidaria, Echinodermata, Porifera, Annelida, Platyhelminthes (B.2.b.iii-ix) -- whose fossil records are central to paleontological interpretation.
Explores accelerator mass spectrometry and dating methods applied to archaeological problems (C.2), including relative and absolute dating techniques.
